q: In one sentence, what is OpenShift's relationship to Kubernetes?
a: OpenShift is a certified Kubernetes distribution — standard K8s objects and kubectl all work unchanged — plus an integrated, self-managing layer of security, developer tooling, networking, and ops features.

q: Why can't you SSH into an OpenShift node and permanently install a package?
a: Nodes run immutable RHEL CoreOS. Manual changes drift the node from its declared state and don't survive. The right way is a MachineConfig, reconciled onto the node pool by the Machine Config Operator.

q: What does `oc get clusteroperators` show, and when is it your first move?
a: The health of the ~30 Cluster Operators that make up the platform (API server, ingress/router, DNS, registry, auth, monitoring…). It's the first thing to check when something platform-level breaks — an Available=False or Degraded=True row points at the culprit.
hint: The platform is built from Operators.

q: What's the difference between a Project and a namespace in OpenShift?
a: A Project is a Kubernetes namespace plus extra metadata and default policy (such as the UID range used by SCCs). `oc new-project` creates an isolated, self-service space.

q: What is a Security Context Constraint (SCC)?
a: An OpenShift admission policy that decides what a Pod may do: run as root? use host network/PID? mount hostPath? add capabilities? which UID/GID range? It both validates a Pod and fills in defaults (like the random UID it runs as).

q: What does the default `restricted-v2` SCC do to your container?
a: Runs it non-root with a random high UID and primary group GID 0, drops most Linux capabilities, and forbids host access. Images that assume root or a fixed UID fail under it.

q: State the arbitrary-UID contract: what three properties must an image have to run under restricted-v2?
a: (1) Don't require a specific UID — it runs as a random one. (2) Make files group-0 owned and group-writable (chgrp -R 0 && chmod -R g=u), because the process's group is 0. (3) Bind only unprivileged ports (≥1024), since non-root can't bind <1024.

q: An image runs locally but CrashLoopBackOffs with 'permission denied' on OpenShift. Likely cause and fix (without anyuid)?
a: It violates the arbitrary-UID contract. Fix the image: make written paths group-0 writable, don't depend on a passwd entry or a specific UID, write to /tmp or a mounted volume, and listen on a port ≥1024 — rather than granting the anyuid SCC.
hint: Fix the image, not the SCC.

q: How do SCCs and RBAC differ?
a: SCCs govern what a running Pod may do (runtime privileges); RBAC governs who can perform which actions on the API. Different axes — you typically need both — and both attach to the Pod's ServiceAccount.

q: What does Source-to-Image (S2I) combine, and what are the two scripts involved?
a: It combines your source code with a language builder image to produce a runnable app image — no Dockerfile. The builder image ships `assemble` (build/install deps into the image) and `run` (start the app).

q: What is a BuildConfig, and what build strategies does it support?
a: The object describing how to build an image: a source (Git/Dockerfile/binary), a strategy, and an output ImageStreamTag. Strategies: Source (S2I), Docker (your own Dockerfile/Containerfile), and Binary (upload local artifacts).

q: What does an ImageStream give you over referencing a registry path directly?
a: Stable tag references that resolve to an immutable image digest (a tag's convenience with a digest's safety), plus image triggers that can automatically redeploy a Deployment when a new image is pushed to a tag.

q: What is the in-cluster DNS name of the OpenShift internal image registry?
a: image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000 — a Service DNS name (<service>.<namespace>.svc) resolving to the registry's ClusterIP, so builds push and Deployments pull without leaving the cluster.

q: Trace an external HTTPS request to an app Pod through its three hops.
a: User → HAProxy router (programmed by a Route, handles host routing + TLS) → Service ClusterIP (kube-proxy/OVN load-balances) → a ready app Pod (a Service endpoint).

q: Name OpenShift's three Route TLS termination modes and where TLS ends in each.
a: edge — TLS terminates at the router, plaintext to the Pod (most common). passthrough — TLS terminates at the Pod; the router can't read the traffic. reencrypt — terminates at the router, then re-encrypts to the Pod with an internal cert.

q: Your app terminates its own TLS and you need it encrypted end-to-end. Which Route termination mode?
a: passthrough — the router forwards the TLS connection untouched so the Pod terminates it. edge won't work because edge decrypts at the router and sends plaintext to the Pod.

q: A Route returns 503. What's the most likely cause and what do you check first?
a: The backing Service has no ready endpoints — usually all Pods are failing their readiness probe or none are running. Check the Service's endpoints and the Pods' readiness before blaming the app. (504 instead means the backend exceeded the route timeout.)

q: What does a NetworkPolicy do, and what's the standard pattern?
a: It turns the default Pod-to-Pod allow-all into allow-list rules (enforced by OVN-Kubernetes). The standard pattern is default-deny ingress for a namespace, then explicitly allow only the required paths (e.g. frontend → backend:8080).

q: Difference between a PersistentVolume, a PersistentVolumeClaim, and a StorageClass?
a: PV = an actual piece of storage in the cluster. PVC = a Pod's request for storage (size + access mode) that binds to a PV. StorageClass = the template a CSI driver uses to dynamically provision a PV when a PVC asks for it.

q: You scaled a Deployment across nodes and Pods are stuck ContainerCreating on volume attach. Why?
a: The PVC's access mode is ReadWriteOnce (RWO), which allows mounting read-write on only one node at a time. Spreading replicas across nodes needs ReadWriteMany (RWX) storage, or a StatefulSet giving each replica its own volume.

q: Are OpenShift/Kubernetes Secrets encrypted? How should you treat them?
a: By default they're only base64-encoded, not encrypted at rest. Treat the manifest as sensitive: gate it with RBAC, never commit real values to Git, and for serious needs layer on the External Secrets Operator or Vault.

q: What two pieces make up an Operator?
a: A Custom Resource Definition (CRD) that adds a new object type to the Kubernetes API, plus a controller that watches those objects and reconciles real-world state to match — encoding Day-2 ops (upgrades, backups, failover) as software.

q: What is OLM, and which objects install and update an Operator?
a: The Operator Lifecycle Manager installs and upgrades Operators. A CatalogSource lists available Operators (OperatorHub); a Subscription says 'install and track this channel'; an InstallPlan applies the changes; a ClusterServiceVersion (CSV) is the installed Operator's manifest.

q: In OpenShift GitOps (Argo CD), how do you roll back a deploy and why is there no config drift?
a: Git is the source of truth, so you roll back with `git revert` and let Argo CD sync. Argo CD continuously compares the cluster to Git and reconciles any difference, so manual or accidental changes are detected and corrected instead of silently persisting.
hint: Git is the source of truth.

q: What are OpenShift Pipelines and OpenShift GitOps, and how do they divide labor?
a: Pipelines is supported Tekton — Kubernetes-native CI where each step runs as a Pod (build/test/image). GitOps is supported Argo CD — declarative deploys from Git. Common split: Pipelines (or S2I) builds the artifact, GitOps deploys it.

q: Give the ordered `oc` ladder for debugging a broken Pod.
a: oc get pods → oc describe pod (read the Events section) → oc logs --previous → oc rsh / oc debug → oc adm top → oc get clusteroperators → oc adm must-gather. Work top-down; most issues are explained by describe's events plus the previous container's logs.
hint: Symptoms → events → logs → exec → resources → platform.

q: Why is checking a database in a liveness probe dangerous?
a: Liveness failures restart the container, so a DB blip would restart every Pod at once — an outage amplified into a restart storm. Keep liveness cheap and dependency-free (is the process wedged?); put dependency checks in the readiness probe, which only removes the Pod from traffic.

q: What does a PodDisruptionBudget protect against?
a: Voluntary disruptions like node drains and cluster upgrades. It keeps a minimum number of replicas available so a rolling node update or upgrade can't evict every Pod of an app at once.

q: Walk through the pipeline an API request passes inside the API server.
a: Authentication (who are you?) → Authorization (may you do this? — RBAC) → Admission (mutating then validating webhooks; an SCC sets the Pod's UID here) → persist desired state to etcd. Controllers/operators/scheduler then watch etcd and reconcile reality to match.

q: A request is rejected with 403 vs 'Pod must run as non-root.' Which stage of the API pipeline produced each?
a: The 403 comes from Authorization (RBAC) — you're not permitted to do the action. 'Must run as non-root' comes from Admission, where an SCC validates the Pod's security context. Knowing the stage tells you whether to fix a RoleBinding or the Pod/image.

q: Taints/tolerations vs node affinity/nodeSelector — which direction does each work?
a: A taint is the node repelling Pods (they need a matching toleration to land there). Affinity/nodeSelector is the Pod attracting itself to certain nodes. To reserve special nodes (e.g. GPU), combine both: taint the nodes AND give the right Pods a toleration plus a nodeSelector.

q: Your three replicas keep landing on the same node. Which scheduling control fixes it?
a: Pod anti-affinity or topology spread constraints, which tell the scheduler to place replicas on different nodes/zones so a single node or zone failure can't take all of them down.
hint: Keep replicas in different failure domains.

q: Name the four autoscalers and what each scales.
a: HPA scales the number of Pod replicas on metrics. VPA right-sizes each Pod's CPU/memory requests. Cluster Autoscaler adds/removes nodes when Pods can't schedule or nodes sit idle. Machine Autoscaler links the Cluster Autoscaler to a specific MachineSet to create/destroy the actual VMs.

q: The HPA wants 20 replicas but new Pods sit Pending. What's missing?
a: Node capacity. The HPA scales replicas but cannot create nodes. The Cluster Autoscaler (driving a MachineSet via the Machine Autoscaler) adds nodes for the Pending Pods to land on. You typically need both working together.

q: Why does 'everything is a CR' make cluster-wide GitOps possible?
a: OpenShift exposes its own configuration as custom resources (OAuth, Proxy, IngressController, MachineConfig, OLM Subscriptions, quotas, SCC bindings…). If it's a CR you can commit it to Git and have Argo CD reconcile it — so the whole platform, not just apps, becomes versioned, reviewable, and rebuildable.

q: With GitOps self-heal on, what happens when someone hand-edits a live resource, and why is that good?
a: Argo CD detects the drift from Git and reverts the live resource back to the committed state. It's good because the cluster can't silently diverge from its source of truth — every real change must go through a reviewed commit, so git revert is your rollback and git log your audit trail.

q: What is the app-of-apps pattern, and what does RHACM add on top?
a: App-of-apps is one root Argo CD Application that points at a Git folder of other Applications, so applying it once bootstraps an entire cluster's config and workloads. RHACM scales the idea to many clusters with a hub-and-spoke model and Policies that enforce/report compliance across the fleet.
hint: One repo bootstraps a cluster; RHACM does fleets.

q: What is the architectural trick that makes Loki cheap, and the trade-off?
a: Loki indexes only labels (namespace/pod/container), not the full log text, and stores raw log chunks in cheap object storage. The small index makes it inexpensive at scale; the trade-off is that searches not anchored by a label must scan more data, so you filter by label first.

q: Describe the OpenShift logging pipeline and its three log types.
a: Vector (a per-node DaemonSet) collects and labels container stdout → a ClusterLogForwarder routes it → LokiStack stores it → you query with LogQL in the console. Logs are split into application, infrastructure, and audit tenants so RBAC controls who can read which.

q: Why should a LogQL query start with a label selector?
a: Because Loki indexes labels, the selector {namespace=...} uses the small, fast index to narrow to the right streams before any line filtering. A bare text search with no labels forces Loki to scan everything — slow and expensive, the exact workload its design avoids.
hint: Narrow by label, then filter lines.

q: How does a service mesh provide mTLS and retries without changing app code?
a: It injects an Envoy sidecar proxy next to every Pod, and all traffic flows through that proxy. The mesh control plane configures every proxy centrally, so mTLS between services, retries/timeouts, traffic routing, and metrics happen in the proxy layer — the application is unaware.

q: What can Knative (OpenShift Serverless) do that a Deployment + HPA cannot, and the trade-off?
a: Knative Serving scales on request concurrency and all the way to zero when idle (an HPA holds a minimum replica count). Great for spiky/event-driven workloads where idle Pods are waste. The trade-off is cold starts: the first request after scaling to zero waits for a Pod to spin up.
hint: Scale to zero vs cold starts.

q: What network contract must every OpenShift/Kubernetes cluster satisfy?
a: Every pod gets its own unique, cluster-wide IP; pods can reach any other pod on any node directly with no NAT; and node agents (kubelet) can reach all pods on their node. The cluster is meant to look like one flat network of pods.

q: Why is a network plugin needed at all — doesn't Kubernetes do pod networking itself?
a: No. Kubernetes defines the network model (the contract) but deliberately delegates the implementation to a pluggable component via CNI. That's why OpenShift can ship more than one engine (OpenShift SDN, OVN-Kubernetes).

q: What is an overlay network, and why does OpenShift use one?
a: An overlay encapsulates a pod-to-pod packet inside an ordinary node-to-node packet so the physical network — which only routes between node IPs — can carry it; the far node decapsulates and delivers it. It's how a flat pod network rides a network that's never heard of pod IPs.

q: Name OpenShift's three IP ranges and what each is for.
a: Cluster (pod) network — IPs for pods (e.g. 10.128.0.0/14); Service network — stable virtual IPs for Services (e.g. 172.30.0.0/16); Machine (node) network — the real NICs of the nodes. Different owners, different lifetimes.

q: What is CNI?
a: The Container Network Interface — a small CNCF standard. A CNI plugin is an executable the container runtime calls with ADD/DEL (plus CHECK/VERSION) to set up or tear down one pod's network namespace: create interfaces, assign an IP via IPAM, install routes.

q: On OpenShift, what chain of components wires up a new pod's network?
a: The kubelet asks the runtime (CRI-O) to create the pod sandbox; CRI-O invokes the configured CNI plugin with ADD; the plugin creates a veth pair, moves one end into the pod namespace as eth0, assigns an IP from the cluster network (IPAM), and adds routes.

q: A pod is stuck in ContainerCreating with a 'failed to set up sandbox network' error. Where do you look?
a: At the network plugin, not your app. That error means the CNI plugin couldn't assign an IP or program the network — investigate the plugin's pods/logs (e.g. in openshift-ovn-kubernetes).

q: What does the Cluster Network Operator (CNO) do?
a: It installs, configures, and continuously reconciles the cluster's networking components based on the Network custom resources. The networkType field (OVNKubernetes or OpenShiftSDN) selects the engine; it's chosen at install and changed only via a migration.

q: What is OpenShift SDN built on, and how does it cross between nodes?
a: Open vSwitch (OVS) on every node, driven by flow rules. Pod-to-pod traffic between nodes uses a VXLAN overlay (UDP 4789): OVS encapsulates the packet node-to-node and decapsulates it on the far side.

q: What are OpenShift SDN's three modes?
a: network-policy (default — enforces Kubernetes NetworkPolicy), multitenant (each project gets a unique VNID and only same-VNID pods can talk; VNID 0 is privileged), and subnet (flat, no isolation).

q: In OpenShift SDN multitenant mode, what is special about VNID 0?
a: VNID 0 (the default project) is privileged: it can reach all projects and all projects can reach it. That's how cluster-wide infrastructure stays reachable while other projects are isolated from each other.

q: What is OVN-Kubernetes, and what is OVN?
a: OVN-Kubernetes is OpenShift's default network plugin. OVN (Open Virtual Network), a sub-project of Open vSwitch, lets you declare a logical network — logical switches, routers, ACLs, load balancers, NAT — which OVN compiles down to real OVS/OpenFlow rules on each node.

q: Walk the OVN control-plane pipeline from desired state to packets moving.
a: Northbound DB holds the desired logical network (switches/routers/ACLs) → ovn-northd compiles it into logical flows in the Southbound DB → ovn-controller on each node reads the SB DB and programs OpenFlow rules into the local OVS. OpenShift's ovnkube components write K8s objects into the NB DB.

q: A NetworkPolicy is enforced everywhere except one node. Which OVN component do you suspect?
a: That node's ovn-controller (or its local OVS). If desired state is right cluster-wide but a single node misbehaves, the per-node controller that programs OpenFlow is the likely culprit — not the NB DB / control plane.

q: Which overlay protocol does OVN-Kubernetes use, and how does it differ from OpenShift SDN's?
a: OVN-Kubernetes uses Geneve (UDP 6081); OpenShift SDN uses VXLAN (UDP 4789). Geneve adds extensible TLV option fields, so the overlay can carry extra metadata — more flexible than VXLAN's fixed header.

q: How does OVN-Kubernetes handle Service load balancing differently from a typical kube-proxy cluster?
a: It implements Services with OVN load balancers in the data plane, so east-west Service load balancing is done by OVN itself rather than a separate iptables/IPVS kube-proxy. One engine owns connectivity, policy, and service LB.

q: Why did OpenShift move from OpenShift SDN to OVN-Kubernetes?
a: OVN-Kubernetes supports things SDN structurally can't: IPv6/dual-stack, egress IP / egress firewall / egress router, IPsec encryption of pod traffic, hardware offload to SmartNICs/DPUs, and hybrid Windows+Linux networking — with one consistent policy model.

q: What's the OpenShift SDN → OVN-Kubernetes status timeline?
a: OVN-Kubernetes has been the default for new installs since OpenShift 4.11; OpenShift SDN was deprecated in 4.14 and removed in 4.17. A cluster on SDN must migrate to OVN-Kubernetes before upgrading past 4.16. (Confirm specifics for your version.)

q: Your cluster is on OpenShift SDN and you want to upgrade to 4.17+. What must happen first?
a: You must migrate to OVN-Kubernetes, because SDN was removed in 4.17 — an un-migrated cluster cannot upgrade past 4.16. The migration is an in-place change of networkType orchestrated by the Cluster Network Operator.

q: What is a Service, and what does ClusterIP give you?
a: A Service is a stable virtual IP plus label-based load balancing over a set of pods. ClusterIP (the default) is an internal-only VIP for east-west traffic — clients hit the stable IP and the plugin spreads connections across healthy pods despite restarts.

q: What is an OpenShift Route, and how does it relate to Ingress?
a: A Route is OpenShift's native resource for exposing a Service externally via the HAProxy-based OpenShift router. It predates Ingress; on OpenShift the Ingress Operator implements Ingress objects by creating Routes under the hood, so Routes are the lower-level primitive.

q: What is the counter-intuitive default behavior of NetworkPolicy?
a: A pod is unrestricted until at least one policy selects it. The moment any policy selects it for a direction (ingress/egress), everything not explicitly allowed in that direction is denied. So 'default allow' flips to 'default deny' per pod, per direction.

q: How is NetworkPolicy enforced, and how do you restrict traffic leaving the cluster?
a: The network plugin enforces it — on OVN-Kubernetes each policy compiles into OVN ACLs (SDN used OVS flow rules). To restrict egress to external IPs/domains, use OpenShift's separate EgressFirewall resource (a plugin feature, not core Kubernetes).
